quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 5 Quiz 2
quiz.subtitle: The Location and Properties of the Incenter
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: The incenter of a triangle is the point of concurrency of which three lines?
questions.explanation: The incenter is the point where the three angle bisectors of a triangle intersect. It is always located inside the triangle.

questions.question: The incenter of a triangle is equidistant from:
options: The three vertices of the triangle
The three sides of the triangle
The midpoints of the three sides
questions.explanation: The incenter is equidistant from all three sides of the triangle. This equal distance is the radius of the inscribed circle (incircle) that touches each side at exactly one point.

questions.question: In any triangle, the incenter always lies:
questions.explanation: Unlike the circumcenter, which can be outside the triangle, the incenter is always inside the triangle regardless of whether the triangle is acute, right, or obtuse.

questions.question: The Angle Bisector Theorem states that a point on the angle bisector is:
options: Equidistant from the two sides of the angle
Equidistant from the two vertices of the angle
Closer to one side than the other
Equidistant from all three sides of the triangle
questions.explanation: The Angle Bisector Theorem states that any point on the bisector of an angle is equidistant from the two sides of the angle. This is why the incenter is equidistant from all three sides.

questions.question: If a point is inside an angle and is equidistant from the two sides of the angle, where must it lie?
options: On the perpendicular bisector of the angle
At the vertex of the angle
questions.explanation: The converse of the Angle Bisector Theorem states that if a point in the interior of an angle is equidistant from the sides, it lies on the angle bisector.

questions.question: The inscribed circle of a triangle has its center at which point?
questions.explanation: The incircle is the circle inscribed in the triangle, tangent to all three sides. Its center is the incenter, and its radius is the distance from the incenter to any side.

questions.question: In $\triangle ABC$, the angle bisector of $\angle A$ meets $\overline{BC}$ at point $D$. If $BD = 6$ and $DC = 9$, and $AB = 8$, what is $AC$?
questions.explanation: By the Angle Bisector Theorem for sides: $\frac{BD}{DC} = \frac{AB}{AC}$. Substituting: $\frac{6}{9} = \frac{8}{AC}$. Cross-multiplying: $6 \cdot AC = 72$, so $AC = 12$.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes the incenter from the circumcenter?
options: The incenter is equidistant from sides; the circumcenter is equidistant from vertices
The incenter is always outside the triangle; the circumcenter is always inside
The incenter is the intersection of medians; the circumcenter is the intersection of altitudes
The incenter is only defined for acute triangles; the circumcenter works for all triangles
questions.explanation: The incenter is equidistant from the three sides (center of incircle). The circumcenter is equidistant from the three vertices (center of circumcircle). This is the fundamental difference between the two centers.

questions.question: The incenter of a triangle is located at the intersection of the angle bisectors. How many angle bisectors are needed to locate it?
questions.explanation: Any two angle bisectors of a triangle will intersect at a single point — the incenter. The third angle bisector necessarily passes through that same point, so only two are needed to locate it.

questions.question: In $\triangle PQR$, the incenter is labeled $I$. If the distance from $I$ to side $\overline{PQ}$ is $4$ cm, what is the radius of the incircle?
questions.explanation: The incenter is equidistant from all three sides, and this distance is the radius of the incircle. Since the distance from $I$ to $\overline{PQ}$ is $4$ cm, the incircle radius is $4$ cm.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the properties of angle bisectors and the incenter, and can distinguish it from the circumcenter.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the basics but review the Angle Bisector Theorem for side proportions and the fact that the incenter is always inside the triangle.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: the incenter is the intersection of angle bisectors, it is equidistant from all three sides, and it is the center of the inscribed circle.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! The incenter is always inside the triangle. It is equidistant from the sides, not the vertices. The Angle Bisector Theorem helps find unknown side lengths.
